nativeRange.habitat: Generally reported from seagrass beds, coastal lagoons, bays, and shallow reef-associated habitats at depths of around 1–11 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical to warm-temperate Western Atlantic; coastal seagrass and reef margins.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on hard-shelled invertebrates in the wild, including oysters, barnacles, mussels, small crabs, and hermit crabs. In captivity, generally accepts meaty frozen foods such as squid, krill, clams, and shrimp. Hard-shelled prey is commonly advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Spiny Box Puffer is a heavily armoured Western Atlantic burrfish with short, permanently fixed spines, generally considered semi-aggressive and best suited to spacious fish-only systems kept by experienced aquarists.
careNotes:
The Spiny Box Puffer (Chilomycterus schoepfii) ranges across the Western Atlantic from Nova Scotia and the New England coast through the Gulf of Mexico to Brazil, and is commonly associated with seagrass beds, coastal lagoons, and shallow reef margins. Unlike many porcupinefishes that erect their spines only when threatened and inflated, the Spiny Box Puffer's spines are short and permanently fixed, giving the body a continuously prickly armour. The species can still inflate by drawing water into a ventral extension of the stomach as a defensive response.

Like all members of the puffer lineage, this species has fused beak-like teeth that grow continuously; offering hard-shelled foods such as clams, snails, and shrimp in shell is generally advised to help keep the beak worn down. It is generally considered an expert-level fish: the combination of large body size, messy carnivorous feeding, sensitivity to water quality, and a tendency to nip at slow or long-finned tankmates makes it ill-suited to community reef tanks. It is not reef-safe and will readily consume invertebrates and corals.

A minimum tank of around 700 L is generally advised, with a long footprint to accommodate the species' active swimming behaviour and need for unobstructed open water. Heavy filtration and frequent water changes are commonly recommended given the species' messy eating habits.

Spiny Box Puffers belong to the family Diodontidae (porcupinefishes), which are generally not considered to carry the strong tetrodotoxin load associated with true pufferfishes of the family Tetraodontidae; as a precaution, however, they are often treated as potentially toxic to tankmates under severe stress or at death. Specimens should not be netted in a way that causes them to inflate with air, as this is commonly reported to be harmful or fatal.
breedingNotes: Breeding is not generally reported to have been achieved in home aquaria. Spawning has been observed in the wild off the New Jersey coast in summer, and juveniles have been reported in the Chesapeake Bay.

primary.notes: Tank-size, water parameters, diet, temperament, reef-safety, and care level taken from the product page. Page lists 180 US gal (~680 L), 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, Expert Only.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Diodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), maximum size (27.9 cm TL), temperature range (18.4–28°C, mean 26.9°C), depth (1–11 m), and Western Atlantic distribution from Nova Scotia/Maine through the Gulf of Mexico to Brazil.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback only. Confirmed family Diodontidae, ~10 inch (~25 cm) adult size, diet of oysters/barnacles/mussels/small crabs/hermit crabs, immovable spines, and the species' ability to inflate. Reef2Reef wiki (designated secondary) returned 403; Wikipedia used as an additional cross-source for taxonomy and diet only.

fish.breedingNotes: Not generally reported to have been achieved in home aquaria; wild spawning observed off the New Jersey coast in summer.
